content:
First observe that the elements of M come in “pairs”  
\[ (3m+1,\;3m+2)\,,\qquad m=0,1,2,\dots \]  
and each such pair sums to  
\[ (3m+1)+(3m+2)=6m+3=3(2m+1)\,. \]  

We want \(2n\) consecutive elements of M whose sum is 300.  Two cases arise:

1.  The block of length \(2n\) starts exactly at the first of a pair.  Then it consists of \(n\) whole pairs, say \(m,m+1,\dots,m+n-1\).  Its sum is
   \[
      \sum_{i=m}^{m+n-1}\bigl(6i+3\bigr)
      =3\,n\,(2m+n)
      \;=\;300
   \;\Longrightarrow\;
      n\,(2m+n)=100.
   \]
   Since \(n\) and \(m\) are non‐negative integers, one finds the only solutions
   \[
     n=2\quad(m=24)
     \quad\text{or}\quad
     n=10\quad(m=0).
   \]

2.  The block starts at the second element of some pair (i.e.\ at \(3m+2\)), alternates into the next pair, etc.  One checks then that the sum becomes
   \[
      3\,n\,(2m+n+1)
      =300
   \;\Longrightarrow\;
      n\,(2m+n+1)=100,
   \]
   whose non‐negative‐integer solutions are
   \[
     n=1\quad(m=49),\quad
     n=4\quad(m=10),\quad
     n=5\quad(m=7).
   \]

Altogether the possible \(n\) are
\[
   \boxed{1,\;2,\;4,\;5,\;10}.
\]

First observe that the elements of M come in “pairs”  
\[ (3m+1,\;3m+2)\,,\qquad m=0,1,2,\dots \]  
and each such pair sums to  
\[ (3m+1)+(3m+2)=6m+3=3(2m+1)\,. \]

1.  The block of length \(2n\) starts exactly at the first of a pair.  Then it consists of \(n\) whole pairs, say \(m,m+1,\dots,m+n-1\).  Its sum is
   \[
      \sum_{i=m}^{m+n-1}\bigl(6i+3\bigr)
      =3\,n\,(2m+n)
      \;=\;300
   \;\Longrightarrow\;
      n\,(2m+n)=100.
   \]
   Since \(n\) and \(m\) are non‐negative integers, one finds the only solutions
   \[
     n=2\quad(m=24)
     \quad\text{or}\quad
     n=10\quad(m=0).
   \]

2.  The block starts at the second element of some pair (i.e.\ at \(3m+2\)), alternates into the next pair, etc.  One checks then that the sum becomes

     n=1\quad(m=49),\quad

     n=5\quad(m=7).

Altogether the possible \(n\) are
\[
   \boxed{1,\;2,\;4,\;5,\;10}.
\]
